methodology.title: How I Work
methodology.subtitle: Innovation Process as a Science
methodology.description: As in any science, my approach is based on two fundamental methods: analysis and synthesis. This allows turning the chaos of unstructured tasks into the precise mathematics of solutions.

steps.title: Step 01. Functional Analysis (As-Is)
steps.text: Breaking down the system into atoms to reveal hidden connections.
details: Analysis begins with decomposition: I identify the SUBJECT (the actor), the OBJECT (the target), and the useful process between them.
The main goal is to define the parameters of the PREFERABLE PATH while identifying harmful functions (HARM) and extra costs (EXTRA COSTS).
At this stage, the PREFERABLY RESULT is formulated, serving as the foundation for projective synthesis.

steps.title: Step 01.1. Projective Synthesis (To-Be)
steps.text: Radical simplification — the path to the Ideal Final Result (IFR).
details: We transition from problem analysis to IFR formulation: the system vanishes, and the function is performed by OVER-SYSTEM resources or the ENVIRONMENT.
We aim to eliminate redundant elements, leaving only the PREFERABLY PROCESS.
When designing from scratch, we work backwards: visualizing the ideal and then identifying tools in the over-system to realize it.

steps.title: Step 02. Fishbone (Causal Analysis)
steps.text: Searching for the root cause, not fighting the symptoms.
details: The Ishikawa diagram (Fishbone) serves as a backbone that connects the chaos of minor factors to the core issue (PROBLEM).
We categorize causes (People, Methods, Machines, etc.) to reveal the complete anatomy of the process.
The vertical structure allows for a deep dive into each 'bone', exposing secondary factors that are often the true root of the problem.
The result is a precise map of risks and bottlenecks, ensuring no critical factor is lost in the overall description.

steps.title: Step 03. System Elevator (Multi-Screen Thinking)
steps.text: Moving the focus from atoms to the entire plant to find hidden resources.
details: For deep understanding, I use 5 levels of detail: from the micro-contact zone to the macro-structure of the entire enterprise.
Focus 1-2 (Micro): Investigating the physical interaction zone and node kinematics — technical contradictions are hidden here.
Focus 3 (Meso): Analyzing 'Man-Machine' ergonomics — identifying control errors and the human factor.
Focus 4-5 (Macro): Looking at workshop logistics and plant infrastructure — here lie the 'free' resources of the supersystem.
This 'elevator' allows not just fixing a part, but optimizing the entire production lifecycle.

steps.title: Synthesis. Altshuller's Table
steps.text: Transitioning from analysis to creating an innovative solution.
details: Once the contradiction is found, it's time for TRIZ tools and Altshuller's matrix.
I select principles that resolve technical contradictions without compromises.
We don't choose the 'lesser evil' — we create a solution where harm turns into benefit.

methodology.conclusion: Conclusion: My method is a path from understanding the function through root cause analysis to the synthesis of an ideal solution. It is engineering that works ahead of the curve.
